sentences.text: Oh, yes.
My mother received six letters from me, all at the same time.
Although I've written many more, a number of other letters.
So they probably arrived later.
But at that time, the post office was in a chaotic situation.
It was after the war, everybody was looking for everybody.
And many people were missing for a long time, for years actually.
And the post office was in a chaos.
Governments changed.
Borders changed.
And so it's understandable that it would take so long to straighten out this sort of system.
But one day I received this precious letter from my mother that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she and Annuska were liberated from Auschwitz on January 27th, 1945, by the Soviet Army.
And they sent them back home.
It would take them-- they didn't get home till March.
It would take them so long to get home on foot, by train, by truck, by farmer's wagon, whatever they found on the road, whatever vehicles.
They would hitchhike, and they would be picked up.
And they would be taken to the next town and so on.
When they came home, they learned that my brother, Michael, my oldest brother, was already home and found no one home and left.
Indeed, he was also liberated by the Soviet Army from slave labor camps and death marches.
And he was also sent home, but he left.
But in those days, survivors in my part of the woods went to train stations.
And people were going back and forth.
And they were looking for each other, for somebody.
And so they hung around, wherever there was train movement.
And that's where he was.
And he heard from somebody who was already home and was going the other way, that mother and sister are already home.
So he headed home.
Now there were the three of them.
My brother Shandor and my father were in Dora-Mittelbau.
And they were liberated by the American Army.
And after some hospitalization, they came home in the fall of 1945.
So my mother and father saw each other again.
My brother, Victor, however, did not survive.
Just three days before liberation, he was beaten to death with the butt of a gun, by an SS.
And he bled to death, according to witnesses.
So you received this letter from your mother?
That's what she-- that's what my mother finally wrote to me.
And at that time, because of my tender age, the Red Cross was trying to unite families.
And they were anxious to send anybody home.
And my relatives in the United States wrote to me that I should write directly to them.
And they would forward the letter to mother.
For it may be important for me to do it this way.
I didn't like the idea.
And I wrote to my mother directly that I need to make a decision about my fate.
I'd like to come home.
I really wanted to see my mother, so desperately.
I guess any 14-year-old under my circumstances would.
Because I was in Sweden.
It's a wonderful country, the people were great.
However, it was a new language for me.
They didn't understand me.
I didn't understand them in the beginning.
Oh, did I learn fast.
It was a crash course in Swedish.
And I spoke, read, and wrote Swedish fluently, before I left there.
I lived there with them for two years.
And one day, my mother wrote to me that as much as we would love to have you come home, we think that you will have a much better life in America.
You see, because the Soviets occupied also my birthplace.
What was Czechoslovakia became Hungary, and what was Hungary now became the Ukraine part of the Soviet Union.
And eventually the Soviet Union closed the borders.
And nobody could go in or out.
And [SIGHS] so mother felt that I'd have a better life here than I would have in the Soviet Union, where there were many hardships to be endured yet, for those who survived.
And so I listened to my mother with pain in my heart, knowing that I would never see her again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But these words were spoken with great love, I know.
[SOBBING] Because only a mother could advise her child under those circumstances not to come home and knowing that we may never see each other again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So my relatives, of course, immediately after learning that I was alive, applied for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually immigration for me.
And prior to that, before I knew about either my American relatives or my family's survival, I felt that if nobody is alive, should there be such an eventuality, I cannot stay in Sweden.

No matter how lovely people are, and how wonderful this country is, I felt that I have to be with my people, and that I want to help build a new land, where discrimination and hatred and prejudice against my people will never be the case.
And so I've-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my Zionist background has come-- has played a big role in that.
I felt that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's the only thing I will accept.
In fact, Youth Aliyah was after me to come on the next boat and the next boat.
There were periodically boats-- I'm not so sure that I can call them ships-- that were not given up to par that were smuggled into Israel at the time.
At that time it was called Palestine.
And as you know, the British was there.
And the White Paper, well, prevented us from entering that country.
And so I was going to go on a ship, if I don't hear from anyone, I was going to go on a ship.
And for some reason, I can't remember why, I just remember telling the gentleman who came over to the house that the next one, the next one will be at [NON-ENGLISH].
If I don't hear by then, I'm going to go with the next ship.
And the ship that I turned down ended up in Cyprus.
It was big banner headlines that the British has captured this ship and all the passengers-- almost all of them former concentration camp inmates-- landed in Cyprus behind barbed wires.
I don't know.
I don't think-- I don't know what the name of the ship was.
But there were others, too, that landed in Cyprus.
Oh, well, the Swedish Red Cross took care of it.
Oh, actually not-- actually they had to work with the organization, the Youth Aliyah, that worked with me on that.
I still have the registration papers, blank.
I did not fill them out.
I just couldn't get myself to fill it out.
Because until I hear from home that somebody was alive, I couldn't accept the fact that nobody survived.
Yes, from Sweden.
That's right.
That's where thousands of us were taken.
And many thousands came after us, many thousands, throughout Sweden.
We had many thousands of survivors.
Some of them stayed.
I know that it was months, it was months.
It could have been five, six months.
It's a long time to wonder if they are alive or not.
But I felt better when I heard that I had my relatives intact.
And that I had all these cousins.
And I remembered the names of each, all these American first cousins.
And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had the Swedish family's blessings.
I lived with them until-- and they helped, they took me-- they saw to it that I get the necessary papers.
I went to Goteborg.
I guess in English it's Gothenburg.
And there I stayed with my Swedish foster father's brother's family overnight.
And I had to present myself in Goteborg.
And came back to Landskrona by myself, with my limited Swedish.
[LAUGHS] And I waited two years for my visa.
Oh, definitely, I attended school.
I couldn't go full time, I was very troubled.
And I had a lot of things to do to get my life in order and my health, to attend to my health.
Very disturbing.
I couldn't close my eyes, literally, without having a nightmare.
Especially with the new anxieties of not being able to-- of not having a family.
I think this was my biggest fear, the biggest fear that I may be the sole survivor of my family.
I don't think-- [SOBBING] I don't think I could have taken that.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I don't know what I would have done.
But I was-- that was-- the very thought was excruciatingly painful.
Each time I thought of it, I tried to distract myself.
And it's so hard, it's not even possible.
Because realistically, I laid the cards on the table.
Gloria, these are your options.
And that's life.
And eventually, when I learned that they are alive, I just couldn't believe that so many survived.

None of the survivors I know-- and I know quite a few, being active, now I'm on the Board of the Holocaust Center of Northern California, this brings me close to many survivors, and I've always been close to survivors.
None of the survivors I know have this many survivors left.
I have no prescription, I have no recipe.
I just think that God was guiding me.
That must be, that must be the reason.
I don't know.
What can I say?
We all had blue eyes, which is true, except for me, I have green eyes.
But you know this may be a joke.
It may sound like a joke.
But I wouldn't be surprised if that wasn't a criteria during the selection, Why, Dr.
Mengele experimented on eyes.
He wanted to turn brown eyes into blue eyes.
And you know, he worked with genetics in his experiments.
Who knows?
I know a woman, a young woman, who comes also from [NON-ENGLISH] who has blue eyes.
And her mother and little brother had brown eyes.
And I'm very serious when I tell you this.
And she arrived, and Mengele wanted to know if she's really her mother.
And says yes, she's my mother.
And so Mengele sent her mother and little brother one side, and Mengele sent her to work and instructed somebody to send her an extra bowl of soup every day.
So that she could survive, because she had blue eyes.
It was very clear and plain.
I thought of that, long before she told me that.
But you know, something sounds so ridiculous.
But nothing is absurd.
Nothing is ridiculous in Auschwitz.
I mean that is Auschwitz.
Because it's so totally opposite of normal.
It's-- anything is possible.
When they want to experiment with genetics, they wanted to produce twins and triplets in the experimental camp, so that 20 years from then, Hitler could have another army.
Because his men were being killed on the Russian front.
So why?
What was the secret to having twins or triplets?
And I think of how we can reproduce now.
He could create an army today, and they all could look like him.
And with the computer age, if something like this happened, why, we are all in trouble.
We must always be careful when it comes to prejudice, racism, and discrimination.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We must stay away from it.
We must treat every human being as human.
This must not happen anywhere.
And then my turn came to come to the US.
And I traveled to Goteborg and took the beautiful ship, [SWEDISH].
And there on that ship, I learned what a grapefruit was and how you eat it, by watching others on the ship.
I never saw such a magnificent fruit before.
And even today, I have half a grapefruit, almost every day.
[LAUGHS] These bring back pleasant memories.
Even oatmeal, which I had at least for four weeks if not six while I was in quarantine in Sweden.
They fed us oatmeal, until our stomachs could expand and get used to more and more foods.
But oatmeal was the staple food.
And I love oatmeal.
Because it represents getting well and approaching a period in my life when I'm going to feel good, when I will enjoy life.
Somehow food represents certain things in our lives.
I landed in New York.
We had a big storm across the Atlantic, so we were two days late.
Funny, that part of the ship was blown away.
And there were several people aboard that were washed off.
It was a big storm.
But actually, I was supposed to take the one previously, the ship that left previously.
And for some reason I missed it, on account of the American consulate in Goteborg.
And so I had to wait for the next one, which is fine.
And I learned that there was an earthquake on the floor of the Atlantic.
And there were a number of people who died.
I just couldn't believe that I was spared so many possible mishaps.
And I survived.
Why me?
But now that I've reached this age, I sometimes wonder is there a reason?
And of course when I began to speak publicly here, about my experiences, in 1977, I sort of believe, I came to believe that I must do this.
I must do this, so that the world will not forget.
It's very important.
It's my mission in life to do this.
I even tell the teachers that I cannot come, unless they can put an entire large group together.
Because survivors are getting on with age.
And we cannot be worn out by traveling far for a small group, we have to be able to reach a number of people.
And of course, this is the reason why I finally submitted to the request of Professor Goldner at San Francisco State Film Department that I must make this documentary film.
And I came to realize that it is important to leave a legacy for future generations.
Sure.
I naturally was very, very anxious to get through that big storm.
Before I left, my Aunt Bella and I agreed-- in letters-- that I would arrive in New York.
And then I will take a train in New York.
And then I will travel to Kansas City, Missouri, where she resides, where she has a home-- she had a home.
And when I arrived at New York Harbor, a telegram waited for me from her, that while I was crossing the Atlantic, my uncle Adolf died.
And here, he and my Aunt Bella sent me the papers.
And they paid for my way to come to the US.
So I was very sad.
And that I should come not to Kansas City but to St Louis, Missouri directly from New York.
And here I arrived in St Louis.
She just got up from sitting shiva, which is a period of mourning for her brother, my mother's brother, only brother.
Before I left New York-- I'm sorry, Europe-- I said, goodbye bloody Europe, I hope I never see you again.
I felt that I have witnessed more than a whole nation could bear, if you could spread it around.
And I'm going to start a new chapter in my life.
But when I found that my uncle was dead, I had to adjust my thinking.
And I remember saying to myself, well, that's really life.
Wherever there are people, people die.
For wherever there are people, they live, and eventually they die.
And so it happened with my uncle.
I lived with my auntie in St Louis for six months, while she settled his estate.
And during that time, I went to Americanization classes, where they taught us English-- language, culture, and history.
And I immediately begin to learn English.
And I was very restless.
My aunt didn't want me to work.
And I wanted to keep busy.
I really wanted to work, to keep my thoughts away from me.

I-- I was just-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had troubles, psychological problems, which I felt due to all this-- the changes, the turmoil, a new language, and the frustration in learning it and making yourself clear.
And school helped a great deal.
I loved it, and I did well.
And eventually, I went to high school in Kansas City and-- Americanization classes and then high school.
And then I finally had five years of high school, in three different countries, in three different languages.
And I decided the hell with it, I'm going to take a college entrance examination.
And I passed it, and I started college.
So I never could finish it, because I could not go full time.
So I have many units.
And each time-- I mean, I took two, three courses at a time, for at least 13 years.
And so I really just wanted to improve myself and catch up.
And I wanted to improve my job, which constantly grew as I grew.
And I worked my way up to research analyst for financial institution.
I worked in that field for 20 years.
Yes.
St Louis or-- Kansas City was the last one.
Oh, while I was in Kansas City, my Aunt Bella decided to go on vacation, to visit Los Angeles.
She hasn't been there since-- since some World's Fair.
And at the same time-- oh, actually, she wanted to go to Tucson, Arizona and then visit Los Angeles at the same time.
And she loved it so much, she decided to move there.
And so this was just fine with me.
Because while I was in Kansas City, Missouri, I met my husband, who was a law student from Hastings College of Law in San Francisco.
And he went home to be with his parents for the summer.
By this time, he had an accounting degree from the University of California.
And so he was going to go to-- he was going to law school.
And he just finished his first year when I met him.
I met-- [LAUGHS] my girlfriend Lucy from Paris, France had a birthday.
And she invited her girlfriends for her birthday on a Saturday night.
Well, when you are 17, Saturday night is special.
And you date and you are almost restricted on the nights when you can go out.
I was.
And so we decided to go and honor Lucy.
So my American girlfriend, [?
Signe ?] [?
Kaufmann ?] and I, went to Lucy's birthday party.
And but what did she do?
Typical of a French girl, of that time I should say, she invited a set of boys of her own.
In the meantime, I was lonely for my boyfriend, Eric.
And there we danced.

And while we were having a good time in this private home, three young men-- who came to Kansas City, one from Harvard, I think medical school, the other one from a Texas University, he was studying engineering, and Carl, from Hastings studying law.
Well, the three buddies came home for the summer, and what to do on a Saturday night?
And the med student, well, actually, his mother heard about the birthday party and told his son, who passed it onto the other two men.
And they crashed the party.
[LAUGHS] And that's where I met Carl.
And when did you get married?
Oh, no, don't rush it.
No, I had to think about this very carefully.
[LAUGHS]
